segments.caption: Your repo → an owned, proven, running cluster
segments.narration: Your infrastructure should be something you own. Running in your own cloud. Proven at every step. This is Alethia — it takes your repository to a running, configured Kubernetes cluster, keyless, and it proves exactly what it did.

segments.caption: Not rented. Not key-holding. Yours.
segments.narration: Most platforms run your infrastructure on their servers, holding your cloud keys, behind their guardrails. Alethia is different. It provisions into accounts you own, it holds zero keys, and every decision leaves portable, cryptographic proof.

segments.caption: Multi-cloud. Keyless. Zero stored credentials.
segments.narration: Connect AWS, Google Cloud, or Hetzner through short-lived federated identity. No access keys are ever written to disk or held in our database. Your projects run across clouds, in accounts that are yours.

segments.caption: Every service and dependency, on one canvas
segments.narration: Every service and every dependency lives on one canvas you can see, configure, and prove. Your network, cluster, databases, caches, DNS, storage — one model, compiled to OpenTofu.

segments.caption: Real resources, honestly modeled
segments.narration: A managed Postgres for orders. Redis for sessions. A private bucket for receipts. On Hetzner, a database becomes an in-cluster Postgres — cloud-honest, never a fake managed service.

segments.caption: Configure in place — no YAML
segments.narration: There's no YAML and no separate form. You configure each resource right here — the cluster, its version, its nodes — and it compiles down to reviewable infrastructure code.

segments.caption: Live cost, before you apply
segments.narration: And it prices itself as you design. Six hundred and twelve dollars a month, from Infracost, updating live — so cost is a decision you make up front, not a surprise on the bill.

segments.caption: Elench — the agent that understands your infrastructure
segments.narration: This is Elench — the agent that understands your infrastructure. It reads your projects, jobs, clusters, and costs through the same tools the console uses.

segments.caption: Ask, or act — you approve
segments.narration: Ask it anything — are my connectors healthy, what's my spend, is anything drifting. Or let it propose an operation. But it never provisions on its own. The model proposes; you approve.

segments.caption: The LLM proposes. The gate disposes.
segments.narration: Because generation isn't the hard part — proof is. Between plan and apply, a deterministic gate verifies the plan. Keyless, least-privilege, no public data stores. Fail-closed: if a control can't be evaluated, it denies.

segments.caption: A signed receipt for every apply
segments.narration: And every approved apply reconciles through GitOps and carries a signed, ed25519 evidence receipt — the plan hash, the controls, the verdict. Prove it once. Then keep proving it, as drift is caught and corrected.

segments.caption: Day-two is the product
segments.narration: This is day-two operations — plan, apply, audit, drift detection — every action a streamed, audited job.

segments.caption: Run it. Stream it. Reconcile it.
segments.narration: Applies run as jobs on a sandboxed runner, with logs streamed live, then ArgoCD reconciles your cluster to Git — continuously.

segments.caption: A self-healing runner fleet
segments.narration: A self-healing fleet of runners executes it all — sized to demand, replacing dead nodes, and rolling itself to new versions with zero downtime.

segments.caption: Own it. Keyless. Proven.
segments.narration: Alethia is open source. Self-host the entire control plane in your own cloud — we host nothing. One place for organizations, roles, and a complete audit trail, so access maps to who actually needs it.

segments.caption: Ship it. Prove it. Keep proving it.
segments.narration: Verified, evidence-backed, keyless provisioning and delivery — that you own. Ship it, prove it, and keep proving it. Create an account, or book a demo, at alethia labs dot I O.
